summary: Steve Yegge announces that his blog is winding down, with only three entries remaining after this one. This installment introduces part three of his 'Programmer's View of the Universe' series as an 18-page science fiction short story set 1000 years in the future. He notes the story will be controversial and offensive to many, emphasizing it doesn't represent his employer's views. The post serves primarily as a teaser for the linked story, with promises that future installments will explain the ideas in traditional blog-rant format.
key_points: Yegge announces he's ending his blog after just three more posts, planning to go anonymous or stop entirely
The story is set 1000 years in the future specifically as a critique of sci-fi's tendency to underestimate project timelines
He's self-aware about the incoming controversy, preemptively distancing himself from his employer
His final planned post about a programming language and Emacs is what he truly cares about, working on it despite physical pain
money_quotes: I'm old and embittered enough to know better. You probably shouldn't even read it. Just wait for someone to summarize it for you.

Unlike 1984, 2001, The Singularity is Near and all the other sci-fi stories that grossly underestimated their project durations, this one is set 1000 years in the future. I.e., right around the corner.
The story is disrespectful to pretty much everyone in the world. It will create a fantastic shit storm.
My final blog-rant entry is the only one I care about anymore. I've been working on it so hard that my fingers have started to fail.
This week is going to suck. People are going to be mad.
key_insight: Even at the announced end of his blogging career, Yegge remains committed to provoking controversy while maintaining a self-aware, sardonic distance from the reactions he knows he'll provoke.
